title: Move.toml uses `rev = "main"` for PAS upstream — not reproducible
description: Both PAS dependencies declared with `rev = "main"`. Move toolchain resolves this to HEAD-at-fetch-time. Move.lock snapshots the current HEAD, but the declared rev is still a moving target. Any CI that skips the lockfile or does a fresh clone pulls the current main.
impact: Audit reproducibility compromised. A reviewer reading Move.toml cannot tell which version was reviewed without inspecting Move.lock in the same commit. If the two drift (e.g. after a dependency bump), the package builds against different code than the lockfile claims.
recommendation: Pin `rev = "b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc"` (current lockfile HEAD) in Move.toml. Establish a policy: every PAS rev bump must be a reviewed commit that updates both Move.toml and Move.lock together.

title: Whitelist read helpers unsafe for non-PAS coin T
description: Public read helpers `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>`, `assert_can_send<T>`, `assert_can_receive<T>` all borrow a whitelist DOF that only exists for PAS-registered T. For non-PAS T, each aborts with a framework-level error. The is_pas_coin-check is missing.
impact: Opaque aborts; harder to debug. An on-chain off-PAS query of `can_send<NonPasCoin>()` aborts the whole PTB.
recommendation: Add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` to each helper. Matches the pattern already established in `policy_cap<T>` (line 272-274).

title: approve_transfer is public and permissionless — ensure invariants with tests
description: `approve_transfer<T>` is public, takes no Auth, and is the sole enforcement point for whitelist compliance on transfers. Any caller with a legitimate `Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` can invoke it. This matches the PAS autoresolve pattern; it is not a bug but elevates the importance of exhaustive negative-path testing.
impact: A silent regression in any of the four assertions (version, amount, pause, send/receive whitelist) would allow non-compliant transfers. Single point of failure for compliance.
recommendation: Add a `tests/pas_transfer_tests.move` that parameterises each negation: (a) stale version, (b) amount=0, (c) mint-redeem paused, (d) sender not whitelisted, (e) receiver not whitelisted, (f) sender whitelisted can_send=false, (g) receiver whitelisted can_receive=false. Each must abort with the expected error code.

title: EMintRedeemPaused error message misleading — transfers are not mint/redeem
description: `EMintRedeemPaused` is abort with message 'Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset' — but the check runs in `approve_transfer`, i.e. blocks a TRANSFER. The error message will confuse a user who attempted a transfer and got told mint/redeem is paused.
impact: Error UX gap. Operators see a mismatched message.
recommendation: Use a more specific error — e.g. `ETransfersPaused` with message 'Transfers are paused for this PAS asset (mint/redeem pause is active)'. Or define a dedicated transfer-pause flag (see R1-009).
evidence:
#[error]
const EMintRedeemPaused: vector<u8> = b"Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset";

title: Unused `mut` binding on treasury_cap in register_pas_asset
description: `mut treasury_cap: TreasuryCap<T>` is declared `mut` but the only use of it is `&mut treasury_cap` as an argument to `pas::policy::new_for_currency(_cap: &mut TreasuryCap<C>, ...)`. After that, treasury_cap is passed by value into `treasury.add_pas_coin_caps`. The `mut` binding is needed only because `&mut treasury_cap` is taken. Move 2024 idiom allows this but it's worth noting the rebinding pattern is correct.
recommendation: Leave as-is — confirmed correct. Add a docstring noting why `mut` is needed (to pass `&mut` downstream).
evidence:
public fun register_pas_asset<T>(
    treasury: &mut Treasury,
    auth: &TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>,
    mut treasury_cap: TreasuryCap<T>,

title: pas_supply::burn creates coin from balance for the sole purpose of calling .burn
description: `burn<T>` receives a `Balance<T>` from `clawback_funds::resolve`, wraps it as a `Coin<T>` via `coin::from_balance`, then calls `TreasuryCap::burn`. A direct `coin::balance_burn<T>(treasury_cap, balance)` or the equivalent framework API avoids the temporary coin creation.
impact: Minor. Slight gas overhead per burn. Temporary Coin<T> object ID allocation.
recommendation: If `sui::coin::balance_burn` (or equivalent) exists in the current framework, use it directly. Otherwise, keep as-is but add a comment.

title: treasury::add_pas_coin_caps allows registering a cap without registering a PAS policy
description: `add_pas_coin_caps` is `public(package)` and stores `TreasuryCap`, `MetadataCap`, `PolicyCap`, and a whitelist Table in the Treasury. It does NOT verify that the PolicyCap corresponds to a Policy<Balance<T>> that has been configured with TransferApproval/ClawbackApproval. If a caller skipped the policy setup (e.g. bypassed pas_admin), the DOFs would be inconsistent: a PolicyCap stored but the Policy un-configured.
impact: Inside the package, `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` is the only caller today, and it always configures the policy before calling this. Risk is constrained to the `public(package)` boundary — future callers could forget.
recommendation: Add a package-level comment requiring callers to configure the policy before storing. Or make this function private and expose only via `register_pas_asset`. Consider `#[allow(lint(public_mut_uid_mut))]` or similar to document invariants.

title: No test for supply::mint/redeem PAS-coin guard
description: The new guard `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinNotAllowed);` in both `mint` and `redeem` has no associated negative test. A future refactor of the PAS storage keys could silently bypass this guard.
impact: If `is_pas_coin<T>` ever returns false for a PAS-registered coin (refactor bug), `supply::mint` would mint PAS coins bypassing whitelist + account flow, creating 'loose' tokens outside the PAS account model.
recommendation: Add tests: (1) register PAS asset T; (2) attempt `supply::mint<T>` with a valid MintPermission Auth; expect abort with `EPasCoinNotAllowed`. Similarly for redeem.

title: PAS events use phantom T but no treasury_id — cross-treasury disambiguation
description: Nine new PAS events are parameterised only by phantom T. Multi-institution indexers relying on event decomposition must correlate T → Treasury via external state. The pattern matches phase-1 events for coin-scoped facts (CoinRegistered<T>, Minted<T>, etc.) — so this is internally consistent.
impact: None intrinsic. Indexer effort grows with institution count.
recommendation: Document in the README or event module docstring that T uniquely identifies the treasury (via OTW uniqueness). Suggest indexer pattern: build a coin-type → treasury-id map from `PasAssetRegistered<T>` events at index-time.
evidence: public struct PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> has copy, drop {}

title: Whitelist Table uses copy-able WhitelistEntry — event emission cost
description: `WhitelistEntry has copy, drop, store`. `copy` + `drop` is fine for a 2-byte (two bool) struct. `store` is required for Table storage. This is idiomatic.

title: Dead DenyCapKey in keys.move — not used by PAS
description: `DenyCapKey<phantom T>` was added for phase-1 regulated coins. PAS assets (per `add_pas_coin_caps`) do NOT register a DenyCap — compliance is via the whitelist. The key remains used by regulated coins, so this is not dead code overall — just noting that PAS and regulated coin paths diverge on this key.
recommendation: No action. Documented for audit record.
evidence:
/// DOF key for DenyCapV2<T>.
public struct DenyCapKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: registerPasAsset script does not verify TreasuryCap has zero supply
description: The TS helper accepts `treasuryCapObjectId` as a string and forwards it. The Move-side `add_pas_coin_caps` enforces `treasury_cap.total_supply() == 0`, but the script has no pre-flight check. A failure surfaces as an on-chain abort (gas paid, PTB failed).
impact: Wasted gas on a doomed registration. Slightly worse operator experience.
recommendation: Query the TreasuryCap object before submission and assert `total_supply == 0`. Fail fast off-chain with a helpful error.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset uses `objectTypes[obj.objectId]` without null check
description: After `const t = objectTypes[obj.objectId] ?? '';` the code branches on `t.includes('...')`. If `objectTypes` is missing an entry (e.g., SDK version changed the response shape), `t` is empty string and every branch silently falls through — the loop never assigns any object ID, and the subsequent assertion aborts with 'Failed to extract IDs' — OK, but the failure message is generic.
recommendation: When `objectTypes[obj.objectId]` is missing, log the unexpected object and its metadata before continuing. Consider throwing early if any entry is missing.
evidence:
const t = objectTypes[obj.objectId] ?? '';
        if (t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')) treasuryCapObjectId = obj.objectId;

title: pas_supply::mint error constants duplicated with supply.move
description: `EMintAmountZero` is defined in BOTH `supply.move` (line 15) AND `pas_supply.move` (line 22), with the same message. Similar for EBurnAmountZero (pas_supply) vs ERedeemAmountZero (supply). Two constants for effectively the same error condition.
recommendation: Move the shared error constants to a single `hadron::errors` module (or to `supply.move` with a `public` visibility) and import from both. Minor refactor.
